volumeTitle: The Little Carthaginian. Pseudolus. The Rope
label: The Little Carthaginian (Section prologue.pr.1 - Section 1.1.169b)

sections.label: Section prologue.pr.1
sections.original: Achillem Aristarchi mihi commentari lubet :
sections.translation: I HAVE a mind [*] to imitate the Achilles of Aristarchus [*] from that Tragedy I ’ ll take for myself the opening : Be silent , and hold your tongues , and give attention . The head-manager it is who bids you listen ,

sections.label: Section prologue.pr.5
sections.original: bonoque ut animo sedeate in subselliis ,
sections.translation: that with a good grace they may be seated on the benches , both those who have come hungry and those who have come well filled . You who have eaten , by far the most wisely have you done : you who have not eaten , do you be filled with the Play . But he who has something ready for him to eat , ’ tis really great folly in him , for our sakes , to come here to sit fasting .

sections.label: Section prologue.pr.11
sections.original: exsurge , praeco , fac populo audientiam ;
sections.translation: Rise up , cryer ! bespeak attention among the people : I ’ m now waiting to see if you know your duty . Exercise your voice , by means of which you subsist and find your clothes ; for unless you do cry out , in your silence starvation will be creeping upon you .

sections.label: Section prologue.pr.15
sections.original: age nunc reside , duplicem ut mercedem feras .
sections.translation: Well , now sit down again , that you may earn double wages . Heaven grant success [*] ! do you obey my commands . Let no worn-out debauchee [*] be sitting in the front of the stage , nor let the lictor or his rods [*] be noisy in the least ; and let no seat-keeper [*] be walking about before people ’ s faces ,

sections.label: Section prologue.pr.20
sections.original: neu sessum ducat , dum histrio in scaena siet .
sections.translation: nor be showing any to their seats , while the actor is on the stage . Those who have been sleeping too long at home in idleness , it ’ s right for them now to stand contentedly , or else let them master their drowsiness . Don ’ t let slaves be occupying the seats [*] , that there may be room for those who are free ; or else let them pay down the money for their places [*] ; if that they cannot do ,

sections.label: Section prologue.pr.25
sections.original: domum abeant , vitent ancipiti infortunio ,
sections.translation: let them be off home , and escape a double evil , lest they be variegated both here with scourges , and with thongs at home , if they ’ ve not got things in due order when their masters come home . Let nurses keep children , baby-bantlings , at home , and let no one bring them to see the Play ;

sections.label: Section prologue.pr.30
sections.original: ne et ipsae sitiant et pueri pereant fame
sections.translation: lest both they themselves may be athirst [*] , and the children may die with hunger ; and that they mayn ’ t be squealing about here , in their hungry fits , just like kids . Let the matrons see the piece in silence , in silence laugh , and let them refrain from screaming here with their shrill voices ; their themes for gossip let them carry off home ,

sections.label: Section prologue.pr.35
sections.original: ne et hic viris sint et domi molestiae .
sections.translation: so as not to be an annoyance to their husbands both here and at home . And , as regards the managers of the performance , let not the palm of victory be given to any player wrongfully , nor by reason of favour let any be driven out of doors , in order that the inferior may be preferred to the good ones .

sections.label: Section prologue.pr.40
sections.original: et hoc quoque etiam , quod paene oblitus fui :
sections.translation: And this , too , besides , which I had almost forgotten : while the performance is going on , do you , lacqueys , make an onset on the cookshops ; now , while there ’ s an opportunity , now , while the tarts [*] are smoking hot , hasten there . These injunctions , which have been given as the manager ’ s command ,

sections.label: Section prologue.pr.45
sections.original: bonum hercle factum pro se quisque ut meminerit .
sections.translation: Heaven prosper them ! troth now , let every one remember for himself . Now , in its turn , I wish to go back to the plot , that you may be equally knowing with myself . Its site , its limits , its boundaries I ’ ll now lay down ; for that purpose have I been appointed surveyor .

sections.label: Section prologue.pr.50
sections.original: sed nisi molestumst , nomen dare vobis volo
sections.translation: But , unless it ’ s troublesome , I wish to give you the name of this Comedy : but if it is an annoyance , I ’ ll tell you still , since I have leave from those who have the management . This Comedy is called the Carthaginian [*] ; in the Latin , Plautus has called it the Pulse-eating Kinsman [*] .

sections.label: Section prologue.pr.55
sections.original: nomen iam habetis . nunc rationes ceteras
sections.translation: You have the name , then ; now hear the rest of the story ; for here will this plot be judged of by you . Its own stage is the proper place for every plot ; you are the critics ; I pray you lend attention . There were two cousins-german [*] , Carthaginians ,

sections.label: Section prologue.pr.60
sections.original: fuere , summo genere et summis ditiis ;
sections.translation: of a very high and very wealthy family . One of them is still alive , the other ’ s dead . The more confidently do I inform you of this , because the undertaker [*] told me so , who anointed him for the pile .

sections.label: Section prologue.pr.64
sections.original: sed illí seni qui mortuost , ei filius ,
sections.translation: But the only son there was of that old man who died , being separated from his father , was stolen at Carthage when seven years old , six years , in fact , before his father died . When he saw that his only son was lost to him , he himself , from grief , fell sick ;

sections.label: Section prologue.pr.70
sections.original: facit illum heredem fratrem patruelem suom ,
sections.translation: he made this cousin-german of his his heir ; he himself departed for Acheron without taking leave [*] . The person who stole the child , carried him off to Calydon , and sold him here to a certain rich old man for his master , one desirous of children , but a hater of women .

sections.label: Section prologue.pr.75
sections.original: emit hospitalem is filium imprudens senex
sections.translation: This old man , without knowing it , bought the son of his host , that same child , and adopted him as his own son , and made him his heir when he himself departed this life . This young man is dwelling here in this house . ( Pointing to the house of AGORASTOCLES .) Once more do I return to Carthage .

sections.label: Section prologue.pr.80
sections.original: si quid mandare voltis aut curarier ,
sections.translation: If you want to give any commission , or anything to be managed — unless a person [*] gives the money , he will be mistaken ; but he who does give it will be very much more mistaken . But this father ’ s cousin of his at Carthage , the old man who is still alive , had two daughters .

sections.label: Section prologue.pr.85
sections.original: altera quinquennis , altera quadrimula :
sections.translation: The one when in her fifth year , the other in her fourth , were lost , together with their nurse , from the walks in the suburbs [*] . The person who kidnapped them , carried them off to Anactorium [*] , and sold them all , both nurse and girls , for ready money , to a man ( if a Procurer is a man )

sections.label: Section prologue.pr.90
sections.original: quantum hominum terra sustinet sacerrumo .
sections.translation: the most accursed of men , as many as the earth contains ; but do you yourselves now form a conjecture what sort of man it is whose name is Lycus [*] . He removed , not long ago , from Anactorium , where he formerly lived , to Calydon [*] here ,

sections.label: Section prologue.pr.95
sections.original: sui quaésti causa . is in illis habitat aedibus .
sections.translation: for the sake of his business . He dwells in that house . ( Pointing to the house of LYCUS .) This young man is dying distractedly in love with one of them , his kinswoman , not knowing that fact ; neither is he aware who she is , nor has he ever touched her ( so much does the Procurer hamper him ); neither has he hitherto ever had any improper connexion with her ,

sections.label: Section prologue.pr.100
sections.original: neque duxit umquam , neque ille voluit mittere
sections.translation: nor ever taken her home to his house ; nor has that Procurer been willing to send her there . Because he sees that he is in love , he wishes to touch this man for a good haul . A certain Captain , who is desperately in love with her , is desirous to buy this younger one to be his mistress . But their father , the Carthaginian , since he lost them ,

sections.label: Section prologue.pr.105
sections.original: mari te rraque úsquequaque quaeritat .
sections.translation: has been continually seeking them in every quarter , by sea and land . When he has entered any city , at once he seeks out all the courtesans , wherever each of them is living ; he gives her gold , and prolongs the night in his enquiries ; after that he asks whence she comes , of what country , whether she was made captive or kidnapped ,

sections.label: Section prologue.pr.110
sections.original: quo genere gnata , qui parentes fuerint .
sections.translation: born of what family , who her parents were . So diligently and so skilfully does he seek for his daughters . He knows all languages , too ; but , though he knows them , he pretends not to know them : what need is there of talking ? He is a Carthaginian all over [*] . He , in the evening of yesterday , came into harbour here on board ship .

sections.label: Section prologue.pr.115
sections.original: pater harunc ; idem huic patruos adulescentulo est :
sections.translation: The father of these girls , the same is the father ’ s cousin of this young man . Now d ’ ye take [*] this ? If you do take it , draw it out : take care not to break it asunder ; pray , let it proceed . ( Moving as if to go .) Dear me ! I had almost forgotten to say the rest . He who adopted this young man as his own son ,

sections.label: Section prologue.pr.120
sections.original: is illi Poeno húius patruo hospes fuit .
sections.translation: the same was the guest of that Carthaginian , this old man ’ s father . He will come here to-day , and discover his daughters here , and this person , his cousin ’ s son , as indeed I ’ ve learnt . He , I say , who ’ ll come to-day , will find his daughters

sections.label: Section prologue.pr.125
sections.original: et hunc sui fratris filium . dehinc ceterum
sections.translation: and this his cousin ’ s son . But after this , farewell !— attend ; I ’ m off ; I now intend to become another man [*] . As to what remains , some others remain who ’ ll explain all to you . I ’ ll go and dress . With kindly feelings do you then recognize me . Farewell ! and give me your aid , that Salvation may prove propitious to you .

sections.label: Section 1.1.129
sections.original: Saepe ego res multas tibi mandavi , Milphio ,
sections.translation: Full oft have I entrusted many matters to you , Milphio ,

sections.label: Section 1.1.130
sections.original: dubias , egenas , inopiosas consili ,
sections.translation: matters of doubt and necessity , and standing in need of good counsel , which you wisely , discreetly , cleverly , and skilfully have by your aid brought to completion for me . For which services I do confess that both your liberty and many kind thanks are due unto you .

sections.label: Section 1.1.135
sections.original: Scitumst , per tempus si obviamst , verbum vetus .
sections.translation: An old adage , if you timely introduce it , is a clever thing : but your compliments are to me what are wont to be called sheer nonsense , and , upon my faith , mere bagatelles [*] . Just now , you are full of kind speeches towards me ; yesterday , without hesitation , upon my back you wore out three bulls ’ hides with flogging .

sections.label: Section 1.1.140
sections.original: Amans per amorem si quid feci , Milphio ,
sections.translation: But if , being in love , I did anything by reason of my distraction , Milphio , it ’ s only reasonable that you should pardon me for it .

sections.label: Section 1.1.141b
sections.original: Haud vidi magis .
sections.translation: I ’ ve seen nothing more reasonable . I , too , am now dying for love ; allow me to thrash you just as you did me , for no fault at all ; and then , after that , do you pardon me being thus in love .

sections.label: Section 1.1.145
sections.original: Si tibi lubido est aut voluptati , sino :
sections.translation: If you have a mind for it , or it gives you pleasure , I do permit it ; tie me up , bind me , scourge me ; I recommend you , I give you my permission .

sections.label: Section 1.1.147
sections.original: Si auctoritatem postea defugeris ,
sections.translation: If , hereafter , you should revoke your permission , when you are unloosed , I myself should be hung up [*] for punishment .

sections.label: Section 1.1.149
sections.original: Egone istuc ausim facere , praesertim tibi ?
sections.translation: And would I venture to do that , to yourself espercially ?

sections.label: Section 1.1.150
sections.original: quin si feriri video te , extemplo dolet .
sections.translation: On the contrary , if I see you but struck , it gives me pain immediately .

sections.label: Section 1.1.151
sections.original: Mihi quidem hercle .
sections.translation: To me , indeed , i ’ faith .

sections.label: Section 1.1.151b
sections.original: Immo mihi .
sections.translation: No , to me .

sections.label: Section 1.1.151c
sections.original: Istuc mavelim .
sections.translation: I could prefer that to be the case . But what now do you wish ?

sections.label: Section 1.1.152b
sections.original: Cur ego apud te mentiar ?
sections.translation: Why need I tell a lie to you ? I am desperately in love .

sections.label: Section 1.1.153b
sections.original: Meae istuc scapulae sentiunt .
sections.translation: My shoulder-blades feel that .

sections.label: Section 1.1.154
sections.original: At ego hanc vicinam dico Adelphasium meam ,
sections.translation: But I mean with this damsel , my neighbour Adelphasium ,

sections.label: Section 1.1.155
sections.original: lenonis huius meretricem maiusculam .
sections.translation: the elder Courtesan that belongs to this Procurer .

sections.label: Section 1.1.156
sections.original: Iam pridem equidem istuc ex te audivi .
sections.translation: For my own part , I ’ ve heard that from yourself already .

sections.label: Section 1.1.156b
sections.original: Differor
sections.translation: I ’ m on the rack with love for her . But than this Procurer Lycus , her master , not dirt itself is more dirty .

sections.label: Section 1.1.159
sections.original: Vin tu illi nequam dare nunc ?
sections.translation: Do you wish now to present him with some mischief ?

sections.label: Section 1.1.159b
sections.original: Cupio .
sections.translation: I should like it .

sections.label: Section 1.1.159c
sections.original: Em me dato .
sections.translation: Why look then , present him with me .

sections.label: Section 1.1.160
sections.original: Abi díerectus .
sections.translation: Go and be hanged !

sections.label: Section 1.1.160b
sections.original: Dic mihi vero serio :
sections.translation: But tell me seriously , do you wish to present him with a plague ?

sections.label: Section 1.1.161b
sections.original: Cupio .
sections.translation: I should like it .

sections.label: Section 1.1.161c
sections.original: Em , eundem me dato :
sections.translation: Well then , present him with this selfsame me ; I ’ d cause him to be having both a mischief and a plague .

sections.label: Section 1.1.163
sections.original: Iocare .
sections.translation: You are joking .

sections.label: Section 1.1.163b
sections.original: Vin tu illam hodie sine damno et dispendio
sections.translation: Should you like this very day , without risk to yourself , to make her free [*] ?

sections.label: Section 1.1.164b
sections.original: Cupio , Milphio .
sections.translation: I should like it , Milphio .

sections.label: Section 1.1.165
sections.original: Ego faciam ut facias . sunt tibi intus aurei
sections.translation: I ’ ll manage for you to make her so . You have indoors three hundred golden Philippean pieces [*] .

sections.label: Section 1.1.166b
sections.original: Sescenti quoque .
sections.translation: Six hundred even .

sections.label: Section 1.1.167
sections.original: Satis súnt trecenti .
sections.translation: Three hundred are enough .

sections.label: Section 1.1.167b
sections.original: Quid iis facturu'sfacturus es ?
sections.translation: To do what with them ?

sections.label: Section 1.1.167c
sections.original: Tace .
sections.translation: Hold your peace . This day I ’ ll make you a present of the Procurer , whole , with all his household .

sections.label: Section 1.1.169b
sections.original: Qui id facturu'sfacturus es ?
sections.translation: What to do ?
